results.possibleIdentity: John H. "Jack" Byrne (born 1946) is a distinguished neuroscientist and former electrical engineer who was active at the Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation (now Northrop Grumman) in Bethpage, Long Island, during the late 1960s. After studying electrical engineering at New York University's Tandon School of Engineering (then NYU Poly), Byrne worked at Grumman as a bio-instrumentation engineer. His work focused on designing and testing electronic circuits and bio-instrumentation for the Apollo Lunar Module, including outfitting astronauts with electrocardiogram (EKG) electrodes and oxygen/CO2 sensors for environmental control system tests. He also worked on early digital computers like the DEC PDP-8 and assisted with simulated launch sequences at Cape Canaveral. Byrne is identified in the records of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) as an engineer volunteer from Grumman interested in the field of electronic circuits. In 1968, he left Grumman to pursue graduate studies in bioengineering at NYU, eventually earning a PhD and becoming a prominent figure in neuroscience. He is currently a professor and researcher known for his work on the cellular and molecular mechanisms of learning and memory.

segment.text: After studying electrical engineering at New York University's Tandon School of Engineering (then NYU Poly), Byrne worked at Grumman as a bio-instrumentation engineer.

His work focused on designing and testing electronic circuits and bio-instrumentation for the Apollo Lunar Module, including outfitting astronauts with electrocardiogram (EKG) electrodes and oxygen/CO2 sensors for environmental control system tests.
He also worked on early digital computers like the DEC PDP-8 and assisted with simulated launch sequences at Cape Canaveral.
Byrne is identified in the records of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)
as an engineer volunteer from Grumman interested in the field of electronic circuits.
In 1968, he left Grumman to pursue graduate studies in bioengineering at NYU, eventually earning a PhD and becoming a prominent figure in neuroscience.
